name: Saga & Distributed Transaction

useCases.use: Order fulfilment spanning inventory, payment, and shipping services
useCases.dontUse: Single-service CRUD operations with local database transactions

useCases.use: Cross-plant inventory transfer requiring multi-system coordination
useCases.dontUse: Read-only reporting queries across multiple data sources

useCases.use: Customer onboarding workflow spanning CRM, billing, and access provisioning
useCases.dontUse: Fire-and-forget notifications where compensation is unnecessary

components: Azure Durable Functions — saga orchestrator with state management
Azure Service Bus — reliable messaging between saga participants
Azure Cosmos DB — saga state store and compensation log
Application Insights — saga execution tracing and failure analytics
Azure Logic Apps — low-code orchestration option for simpler sagas

guardrails.rule: Every saga step must have a defined compensating action
Saga orchestrator must implement timeout for each participant step
Saga state must not contain PII or payment card data
Saga execution must be fully traceable via correlation IDs
Prefer choreography for simple 2-step flows; use orchestration for 3+ steps

revisionHistory.author: David Okonkwo
revisionHistory.summary: Initial saga pattern with Durable Functions orchestration and compensation guidance

strategicIntent: Define orchestration and compensation patterns for managing distributed transactions across multiple microservices, ensuring eventual consistency and graceful failure handling without two-phase commit.
exceptionProcess: Submit Integration Architecture Board request for distributed transaction approaches outside the saga pattern. Must include consistency analysis, failure mode documentation, and data loss risk assessment.
interactionSteps: Saga orchestrator receives initiating command (e.g., 'Place Order')
Orchestrator sends command to first participant (inventory reservation)
Participant executes local transaction and publishes completion event
Orchestrator advances to next step (payment processing) on success
On participant failure, orchestrator initiates compensation chain in reverse order
Each compensating action is logged in Cosmos DB for audit and replay
Saga completes with final status event published for downstream consumers
problemStatement: Multi-step business processes spanning order management, inventory reservation, payment processing, and logistics require transactional guarantees, but distributed two-phase commit is impractical across independent microservices and external partner systems.
securityConsiderations: Saga state store must be encrypted at rest and access-restricted to orchestrator identity
Compensation actions must be idempotent to handle retry scenarios safely
External partner steps must use timeout-based compensation, not indefinite waits
Saga correlation IDs must be propagated across all participants for end-to-end tracing
Sensitive data (payment details, PII) must not be stored in saga state — use references only
